title: Field Sampling and Laboratory Analysis of Microzooplankton in Small Wetlands with Sanitary-Biological Assessment
author: Evhenii Hordiienko

steps_raw.instruction: Record exact GPS coordinates 50.991293, 34.473122. Describe water depth (0.3–0.8 m), vegetation (sedges, sphagnum, duckweed), water colour, peat layer, and visible disturbances. Document the site and each micro-biotope via photography.

Collect 3–5 samples (0.5–1 L each) from different micro-biotypes (open water, among duckweed/sedges, near sphagnum, bottom detritus). Gently submerge containers to collect water with surface detritus, plant fragments, and sediment. Label immediately and transport in shade; process within 2–4 hours.
Allow samples to settle at room temperature for 30–90 minutes. It is critical not to stir or agitate the containers after the settling period.
Using a pipette, take subsamples from the surface, middle, and bottom layers. Place 1–2 drops on a slide and apply a coverslip. Prepare 5–10 slides per sample to ensure representative coverage.

Conduct initial screening under a stereomicroscope (10–40×) for larger organisms (Daphnia, insect larvae). Transition to a compound microscope at 100× for overview, and 200–400× for specific identification. Record taxa, relative abundance, and observed behavior.

Identify organisms using standard keys (e.g., Foissner for ciliates, Koste for rotifers). Focus specifically on Gastrotricha and free-living Nematoda_. Document findings with photos and video recordings.

Evaluate saprobity based on dominant groups. High numbers of saprophytic ciliates (_Paramecium, Vorticella, Stentor_), rotifers, nematodes, or gastrotrichs should be used to classify the saprobity level (e.g., β-mesosaprobic with α-elements).
Record all raw data and observations. If necessary, fix subsamples using 4% formaldehyde or Lugol’s solution. Archive all digital images and field notes.
